name: Obatala
description: Obatala is the king of white cloth, the oldest of all orishas, and the sculptor of humanity. Sent by Olodumare to create the earth, Obatala descended from heaven on a golden chain carrying a snail shell of sand, a white hen, a black cat, and a palm nut. He poured sand on the primordial waters and the hen scratched it into dry land. He then molded the first humans from clay. Tricked by Eshu into drinking palm wine, he created some imperfect bodies while drunk, and Olodumare decreed that people with disabilities would be sacred and under Obatala's protection. He is the patron of the physically challenged.
appearance: Elderly figure dressed entirely in white, carrying white chalk (efun) and a staff. Wears a white conical crown. Serene, pure, and dignified. Often depicted holding white snails or clay.
behavior: Peaceful, just, and pure. Creates human bodies from clay. Protects the disabled and deformed. Forbids palm wine and alcohol. Mediates disputes. Embodies wisdom, patience, and purification.
habitat: Heavens (Orun), white sacred groves, Ile-Ife, the sky
cultural_significance: Obatala is one of the most revered orishas in the Yoruba pantheon and its head in many traditions. His worship centers in Ile-Ife and Ejigbo. As Oxala in Candomble, he is syncretized with Jesus Christ. In Cuban Santeria/Lukumi, Obatala (also Obbatala) is the father of all orishas and is associated with peace, purity, and wisdom. He is syncretized with the Virgen de las Mercedes (Our Lady of Mercy). All white attire is sacred to him. His devotees wear white and observe strict taboos against palm wine and alcohol.
summary: Obatala, the King of White Cloth, is the oldest Yoruba orisha who sculpted humanity from clay — the creator of human bodies and protector of the physically different.
fun_fact: Obatala, the King of White Cloth, is the oldest Yoruba orisha who sculpted humanity from clay — the creator of human bodies and protector of the physi
source: Yoruba religion — Obatala is the creator deity documented in traditional Ifá corpus.
seo_description: Obatala, the King of White Cloth, is the oldest Yoruba orisha who sculpted humanity from clay — the creator of human bodies and protector of the physi

name: Oya
description: Oya is the powerful Yoruba orisha of winds, storms, lightning, transformation, and the Niger River. Her name derives from "o ya" meaning "she tore." She is the only orisha who can control the Egungun (spirits of the dead), a power granted by Babalú Ayé. Known as the "mother of nine" for her nine stillborn children, Oya guards the gates of the cemetery and escorts souls to the afterlife. She is the goddess of the marketplace, change, and rebirth. Married first to Ogun, then to Shango, she and Shango together represent the full force of the storm — her wind precedes his thunder.
appearance: Fierce warrior woman with a sword/machete in one hand and a flywhisk (iruke) in the other. Wears nine multi-colored scarves around her waist. Associated with the water buffalo. Can transform into a buffalo or tornado.
behavior: Fierce, protective, and transformative. Controls winds and storms. Guides souls of the dead. Protects the marketplace. Brings sudden change. Fights alongside Shango in battle. Cannot stand the sight of a ram (due to betrayal).
habitat: Marketplaces, cemetery gates, the Niger River (Odo Oya), storm clouds, crossroads between life and death
cultural_significance: Oya is central to Yoruba and diaspora religions. In Santería and Candomblé, she is syncretized with Saint Barbara and celebrated on February 2. She represents the inevitability of change and the courage to face transformation.
summary: Oya is the Yoruba orisha of winds, storms, death, and rebirth — the fierce warrior who guards the cemetery gates and sweeps away the old to make way for the new
fun_fact: Oya is the Yoruba orisha of winds, storms, death, and rebirth — the fierce warrior who guards the cemetery gates and sweeps away the old to make way f
source: Yoruba oral tradition, Wikipedia, Santeria Church of the Orishas
seo_description: Oya is the Yoruba orisha of winds, storms, death, and rebirth — the fierce warrior who guards the cemetery gates and sweeps away the old to make way f

name: Orunmila
description: Orunmila is the Yoruba orisha of wisdom, divination, knowledge, and prophecy. He is the custodian of the Ifá divination system, which contains 256 sacred verses (Odu) that encode the entire knowledge of the universe. Present at creation, Orunmila witnessed everything that would happen and recorded it in the Ifá corpus. He taught humanity the art of divination through Babalawos (fathers of secrets), the initiated priests who interpret sacred palm nuts and the divination chain. Orunmila represents destiny, fate, and the power of knowledge. UNESCO recognized the Ifá divination system as a Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity in 2005.
appearance: Elderly, serene man with a staff. Usually depicted in green and yellow. Carries the divination chain (opele) and sacred palm nuts (ikin). Surrounded by symbols of wisdom and learning.
behavior: Wise counselor and prophet. Reveals destiny through divination. Advises humans on their fate. Does not participate in wars or conflicts. Speaks truth without bias. Key mediator between humans and Olodumare.
habitat: Temples of Ifá, sacred groves, divination chambers, the heavens
weaknesses: cannot change fate, only reveal it
cultural_significance: Orunmila is essential to Yoruba religion — no ceremony begins without Ifá divination. His Babalawos are among the most respected spiritual specialists. The Ifá system is a complete philosophical, medical, and spiritual knowledge system.
summary: Orunmila is the Yoruba orisha of wisdom and divination, the keeper of the Ifá oracle who witnessed creation and reveals the destiny of all beings.
fun_fact: Orunmila is the Yoruba orisha of wisdom and divination, the keeper of the Ifá oracle who witnessed creation and reveals the destiny of all beings.
source: Yoruba oral tradition, Wikipedia, UNESCO documentation
seo_description: Orunmila is the Yoruba orisha of wisdom and divination, the keeper of the Ifá oracle who witnessed creation and reveals the destiny of all beings.

name: Oduduwa
description: Oduduwa is the legendary progenitor of the Yoruba people, first Oba (king) of Ile-Ife, and an orisha of creation. According to tradition, Oduduwa descended from heaven on a chain carrying the materials of creation and completed the work Obatala had begun, creating dry land. His sixteen sons became the founders of the principal Yoruba kingdoms, including Oyo, Benin, Ila, and Ketu. Every crowned Oba in Yorubaland traces their lineage to Oduduwa. The Ooni of Ife is considered his direct descendant and the spiritual head of all Yoruba people. As the initiator of the Ogboni society, he established the sacred authority of Yoruba kingship.
appearance: Regal king wearing a beaded crown (adenla) with veil. Often depicted as both male and female in different traditions (the primordial Oduduwa represents the Divine Feminine aspect). Seated on a throne with royal attendants.
behavior: Founder and unifier of the Yoruba people. Creator king who establishes order. Competes with Obatala for authority. Represents divine right of kings and sacred kingship.
habitat: Ile-Ife (the cradle of Yoruba civilization), the royal palace, sacred groves
cultural_significance: Oduduwa is the foundational figure of Yoruba identity and kingship. The Oduduwa Day festival and the Ooni of Ife's throne remain central to Yoruba cultural and political identity. His dynasty is among the oldest continuous monarchies in Africa.
summary: Oduduwa is the progenitor of the Yoruba people and first king of Ile-Ife — the divine founder who established Yoruba civilization and the sacred authority of kings.
fun_fact: Oduduwa is the progenitor of the Yoruba people and first king of Ile-Ife — the divine founder who established Yoruba civilization and the sacred autho
source: Yoruba oral tradition, Wikipedia, scholarly sources

name: Olodumare
description: Olodumare is the Supreme Being and ultimate creator in Yoruba religion. Also known as Olorun (Owner of Heaven), Olodumare is the source of all existence, the giver of ase (divine authority), and the architect of the universe. Too vast and transcendent for direct human worship, Olodumare has no shrines, priests, or sacrifices — instead communicating through the orishas who serve as intermediaries. Olodumare created the cosmos, established the framework of existence, and breathed life into the clay figures that Obatala molded. Every orisha is an emanation of Olodumare. The name derives from "O ní odù mà rè" — the owner of the source of creation that does not become empty.
appearance: No physical form — too immense for human perception. Sometimes conceptualized as the sky, the sun, or infinite space. Represented by the number 401 (symbolizing infinity). Associated with the sun and heaven.
behavior: Transcendent and omnipotent. Creates and sustains all existence. Does not intervene directly in human affairs. Delegates authority to orishas. Governs through cosmic order. All-knowing and all-powerful but distant.
habitat: The heavens (Orun), beyond the physical universe, the sun
cultural_significance: Olodumare is the foundation of Yoruba theology. The concept of a transcendent supreme being who delegates to lesser divinities is central to understanding the Yoruba cosmological hierarchy. The name is used in Christian and Muslim Yoruba communities for God.
summary: Olodumare is the Supreme Being of the Yoruba religion — the transcendent creator of the universe who delegates divine authority to the orishas
fun_fact: Olodumare is the Supreme Being of the Yoruba religion — the transcendent creator of the universe who delegates divine authority to the orishas.
source: Yoruba oral tradition, Wikipedia, scholarly works on Yoruba religion
seo_description: Olodumare is the Supreme Being of the Yoruba religion — the transcendent creator of the universe who delegates divine authority to the orishas.

name: Ogo (The Cosmic Fox)
description: Ogo (The Cosmic Fox) is a spirit from Mali folklore. Also known as ogo. It is said to dwell in The Void, terrestrial forests. In folklore, it is described as attempted to seize creative power for himself, introducing chaos and disorder into the universe. Represents the necessity of disorder for a dynamic and evolving universe.
appearance: A fox; capable of shapeshifting into various forms.
behavior: Attempted to seize creative power for himself, introducing chaos and disorder into the universe.
cultural_significance: Represents the necessity of disorder for a dynamic and evolving universe.

fun_fact: Represents the necessity of disorder for a dynamic and evolving universe.

name: Zangbeto
description: Zangbeto, meaning 'Men of the Night' in the Goun and Fon languages, are sacred night guardian spirits central to the Vodun faith of Togo and Benin. These towering figures made of raffia and palm fibers spin through villages at night, serving as the eyes of justice in the darkness. According to tradition, Zangbetos are living vessels of ancestral spirits. Believers maintain that no human is inside the raffia structure, only spirit. They function as an unofficial spiritual police force, guarding against thieves and witches. The Zangbeto's hypnotic spinning is believed to cleanse the community of evil.
appearance: Zangbeto takes the form of a guardian-spirit known in Benin folklore.
behavior: Zangbeto is night, guardian, guard, appear, social in Benin folklore.
cultural_significance: Represents ancestor veneration and community security. Vodun tradition.
summary: Towering raffia night guardians of Togo's Vodun faith that spin through villages protecting communities and dispensing impartial justice

seo_description: Zangbeto are towering raffia night guardian spirits of Togo's Vodun faith that spin through villages protecting communities and dispensing justice.
source: Benin/Gun traditional practice — Zangbeto night masquerade documented in Fon ethnography.

name: Zin Kibaru
description: Zin Kibaru is a water spirit from Songhay-Zarma mythology of the Niger River region. The term Zin refers to a class of genies inhabiting water bodies, and Kibaru specifically designates a powerful water demon that demands respect and offerings. These spirits are associated with the Holeys, possession dances of the Songhay-Zarma people. Zin Kibaru are believed to inhabit the deepest parts of the Niger River and its tributaries. Fishermen and boatmen must make proper offerings before venturing onto certain waters. The Zin spirits can capsize boats, drown individuals, or bring abundant fish depending on how they are treated. They are served by the Do and Karou priestly lineages.
appearance: Powerful water genie, sometimes described as a massive aquatic creature or a humanoid with water-associated features.
behavior: Guards water bodies, demands respect and offerings. Rewards proper conduct with good fishing. Punishes disrespect with drowning or boat capsizing.
habitat: Deep waters of the Niger River and its tributaries in Niger
weaknesses: must be managed by Do and Karou priests
cultural_significance: Zin Kibaru belief regulates fishing practices along the Niger River and maintains the authority of traditional priestly lineages.
summary: Powerful water genie from Songhay-Zarma mythology of Niger that inhabits the Niger River and demands respect from fishermen
fun_fact: Zin Kibaru belief regulates fishing practices along the Niger River and maintains the authority of traditional priestly lineages.
seo_title: Zin Kibaru – The Water Genie of the Niger River
seo_description: Zin Kibaru is a powerful water genie from Songhay-Zarma mythology of Niger, inhabiting the Niger River and controlling the fortunes of fishermen.

name: Nyame
description: Nyame (Onyame) is the supreme god of the Akan people (Ashanti, Fante, Akuapem) of Ghana. Also known as Nyankopon or Odomankoma, Nyame is the omniscient, omnipotent sky deity who created the universe and all living things. The name means "the one who knows and sees everything." Nyame is part of a trinity with Nyankopon (his life-giving power) and Odomankoma (his creative force). According to tradition, Nyame once lived on earth but withdrew to the heavens after being struck by a woman's pestle while pounding fufu. The Adinkra symbol "Gye Nyame" ("I Fear None Except God") is one of the most recognized symbols of Akan culture. Nyame is represented by the sky, and his sacred day is Saturday.
appearance: No fixed physical form — represented by the sky, the sun, and the vast firmament. Sometimes depicted as an old man in the sky. The Gye Nyame symbol is the primary visual representation.
behavior: Distant and transcendent creator. Does not intervene directly in daily human affairs. Delegates authority to the abosom (lesser deities) and ancestors. All-knowing and all-powerful. Must be invoked first in prayer.
cultural_significance: Nyame is the foundation of Akan religion. The Gye Nyame symbol is ubiquitous in Ghanaian art and architecture. Saturday is Nyame's sacred day, and Saturday-born children are considered specially connected to the deity.
summary: Nyame is the supreme sky god of the Akan people — the omniscient creator who withdrew to the heavens and whose symbol "Gye Nyame" is the most recognized in Ghanaian culture.
fun_fact: Nyame is the supreme sky god of the Akan people — the omniscient creator who withdrew to the heavens and whose symbol "Gye Nyame" is the most recogniz
source: Akan/Ashanti oral tradition, Wikipedia, Akan religion scholarship

name: Ntikuma
description: Ntikuma is the son of Anansi the spider in Akan folklore. Unlike his cunning father, Ntikuma often demonstrates wisdom that surpasses Anansi's schemes. In the famous story "Anansi and the Pot of Wisdom," when Anansi tried to hide all the world's wisdom in a pot at the top of a tree, it was young Ntikuma who pointed out that tying the pot in front made climbing impossible — suggesting instead that Anansi tie it on his back. Angered that his son had outsmarted him, Anansi threw down the pot, shattering it and scattering wisdom across the world. Ntikuma represents the wisdom of youth and the Akan belief that cleverness can come from unexpected sources, including children.
appearance: Young Akan boy, sometimes depicted with spider-like features. Playful expression. Often shown pointing or laughing — the child who sees what adults miss.
behavior: Wise beyond his years. Observes quietly and offers simple but profound solutions. Takes joy in outsmarting his boastful father Anansi. Represents humility and the wisdom of innocence.
habitat: Akan villages, Anansi's household, the everyday world of Akan folktales
cultural_significance: Ntikuma appears in key Anansi stories and reinforces the Akan value that wisdom can come from anyone, regardless of age. The story of the pot of wisdom is one of the most widely told Anansi tales across West Africa and the Caribbean.
summary: Ntikuma is the wise son of Anansi in Akan folklore — the child who outsmarted his father and taught that wisdom belongs to everyone, not just the clever.
fun_fact: Ntikuma appears in key Anansi stories and reinforces the Akan value that wisdom can come from anyone, regardless of age. The story of the pot of wisdom is one of the most widely told Anansi tales across West Africa and the Caribbean.
source: Akan folklore; Ntikuma is the clever son of Anansi, the spider trickster.
seo_description: Ntikuma is the wise son of Anansi in Akan folklore — the child who outsmarted his father and taught that wisdom belongs to everyone, not just the clev

name: Nne Miri
description: Nne Miri (literally "Mother of Waters") is the Igbo spirit of rivers, streams, and bodies of water. She is the patroness of fishermen and the guardian of marine life. Closely related to the pan-African Mami Wata figure, Nne Miri is a beautiful but dangerous water spirit who can grant wealth and fertility to those she favors but can also drown those who offend her. She is considered a matron spirit for specific professional guilds, particularly those associated with fishing and water-based trade. Nne Miri is sometimes propitiated with offerings at riverbanks before fishing expeditions or before crossing dangerous waters. She represents both the life-giving and destructive power of water.
appearance: Beautiful woman with long flowing hair, often with a fish tail (mermaid form). Carries a mirror and a comb. Can appear as an attractive woman on the riverbank who lures men into the water.
behavior: Protective of rivers and marine life. Rewards those who respect her waters. Can grant children to barren women and wealth to fishermen. Drowns those who pollute her waters or offend her.
habitat: Rivers, streams, lakes, ponds, and other bodies of fresh water
cultural_significance: Nne Miri is part of a broader West African water spirit tradition that includes the Yoruba Yemoja and the pan-African Mami Wata. She represents the sacredness of water bodies in Igbo cosmology.
summary: Nne Miri is the Igbo mother of waters — the river spirit who grants fertility and abundance but punishes those who disrespect her aquatic domain.
fun_fact: Nne Miri is the Igbo mother of waters — the river spirit who grants fertility and abundance but punishes those who disrespect her aquatic domain.
source: Igbo cosmology — Nnemiri water spirit of the Niger River delta.
