source: Wikipedia

name: Bemba
description: Bemba (also called Maa Ngala or simply Ngala) is the supreme creator god of the Bambara people of Mali who created himself as a quaternity of four beings: Pemba (male, earth/air), Mousso Koroni Koundye (female, fire), Faro (female, water), and Ndomadyiri (male). Hermaphroditic in nature, Bemba encompasses both masculine and feminine creative principles. He existed before all things as Koni, the primordial energy, and brought the universe into being through a series of emanations. Bemba is distant and all-powerful, approached through the six initiation societies that form the backbone of Bambara spiritual life.
appearance: Invisible, all-encompassing. Manifested as the quaternity of Pemba, Mousso Koroni, Faro, and Ndomadyiri. No physical form, symbolized by the number four.
behavior: Created the universe through self-emanations. Distant from daily affairs but maintains cosmic order. Communicates through initiation societies and ritual.
habitat: The cosmos, the sky, spiritual realm
cultural_impact: The six initiation societies of the Bambara (N'domo, Komo, Nama, Kono, Tyiwara, Kore) are considered direct paths to understanding Bemba's cosmic order, guiding members from childhood to full spiritual maturity.
symbolism: The number four; the quaternity; hermaphroditic divine nature; the six initiation stages
fun_fact: Bemba is described as a quaternity — a four-in-one deity — rather than a trinity, embodying the four elements (air, fire, water, earth) and both genders, making him one of the most structurally complex creator gods in African mythology.
summary: Bambara supreme creator god manifesting as a quaternity of four elemental beings
source: Marcel Griaule, Conversations with Ogotemmêli (1948)
cultural_significance: Bemba is a a deity from West Africa mythology, representing important cultural values and traditional beliefs.

name: Bolukun
description: Note: This entry is being added with slug bolukun-yoruba but may be a variant of Olokun, who already exists in the database. Bolukun is a regional Yoruba variant name for the orisha of the ocean depths. Bolukun represents the unfathomable depths of the sea and the mysterious, untamed aspects of water. As the ruler of the great waters, Bolukun controls the tides, marine life, and the hidden treasures of the ocean floor. This entity is often considered the source of extreme wealth from the sea (including the slave trade era wealth) but also represents the danger and unpredictability of deep waters. Offerings are made to Bolukun for safe sea voyages and abundant fishing.
appearance: Enormous, majestic figure emerging from the depths. Partially hidden by waves. Wears coral and pearls. Carries a paddle or oar. Surrounded by sea creatures and shimmering treasures.
behavior: Ruler of the ocean depths. Both generous and terrifying. Controls sea wealth and marine abundance. Can capsize boats and cause storms when angered. Demands respect and proper offerings.
habitat: The deepest parts of the ocean, underwater palaces
cultural_significance: Bolukun/Olokun represents the power and mystery of the Atlantic Ocean that connects West Africa to its diaspora. In Brazil, Olokun is associated with the depths of the unconscious mind.
summary: Bolukun is the Yoruba orisha of the ocean depths who rules the untamed waters and guards the hidden wealth beneath the sea.
fun_fact: Bolukun is the Yoruba orisha of the ocean depths who rules the untamed waters and guards the hidden wealth beneath the sea.
source: Yoruba religion — Bolukun sky orisha in the traditional Ifá corpus.
seo_description: Bolukun is the Yoruba orisha of the ocean depths who rules the untamed waters and guards the hidden wealth beneath the sea.

name: Bida Serpent
description: Bida is the sacred seven-headed serpent of the Mande people, associated with the ancient Ghana Empire (Wagadu). According to legend, the prosperity of Wagadu depended on the serpent Bida, who demanded an annual sacrifice of a virgin maiden in exchange for abundant gold and three days of rain each year. The serpent lived in a well or cave near the capital, Koumbi Saleh. The pact was broken when a brave young man named Mamadi Sefe Dekote (or Amadu Sefe) rescued the intended sacrifice by decapitating Bida. As the serpent died, it cursed the land — the gold disappeared, the rains failed, and Wagadu declined. The Bida serpent story explains the fall of the Ghana Empire and the rise of the Mali Empire under Sundiata. It is a foundational myth of Mande history.
appearance: Enormous seven-headed serpent. Coiled around the gold mines or within a sacred well. Scales shimmering with gold. Each head represents a different aspect of the empire's power.
behavior: Sacred guardian of Wagadu's prosperity. Demands sacrifice for continued blessings. Curses the land when killed. Represents the dangerous pact between civilization and nature.
habitat: Sacred well or cave near Koumbi Saleh (capital of Ghana Empire), the gold mines of West Africa
cultural_significance: The Bida serpent myth preserves the historical memory of the Ghana Empire's gold trade and decline. It serves as a cautionary tale about the costs of prosperity and the fragility of imperial power. The story is interwoven with the Epic of Sundiata.
summary: Bida is the seven-headed serpent of Wagadu in Mande mythology whose annual sacrifice brought gold and rain — until a hero broke the pact and cursed the Ghana Empire.
fun_fact: The Bida serpent myth preserves the historical memory of the Ghana Empire's gold trade and decline. It serves as a cautionary tale about the costs of prosperity and the fragility of imperial power. The story is interwoven with the Epic of Sundiata.
source: Mande oral tradition — Bida rainbow serpent central to the Sundiata epic.

name: Bouki (Hyena)
description: Bouki the Hyena is the greedy, foolish, and constantly hungry foil to Leuk the Hare in Wolof folklore. Bouki is the classic trickster's dupe — always falling for Leuk's schemes due to her insatiable appetite and lack of foresight. In each tale, Bouki's greed, laziness, and carelessness lead to her downfall. Whether building a ladder, making a cooking pot, or thatching a roof, Bouki rushes her work while Leuk does it properly — and Bouki inevitably suffers the consequences. Bouki also appears in stories with other characters, such as the old woman whose magic horn she borrows and never returns. Despite her constant failures, Bouki never learns, making her the perfect vehicle for Wolof moral lessons about the consequences of greed, laziness, and dishonesty.
appearance: Large, shaggy hyena with a perpetually hungry expression. Limps slightly from her many falls. Often shown with a drooping tongue and foolish, desperate eyes.
behavior: Greedy, lazy, and careless. Always hungry. Falls for Leuk's tricks every time. Rushes work and cuts corners. Never learns from her mistakes. Blames others for her misfortunes.
habitat: The savanna, Senegalese bush, near villages (looking for food)
cultural_significance: Bouki is the essential counterpoint to Leuk in Wolof storytelling. Together they form a classic trickster pair that has counterparts across West Africa. Bouki's repeated failures teach Wolof children the value of patience, careful work, and honesty.
summary: Bouki the Hyena is the greedy, foolish dupe of Wolof folklore whose constant hunger and carelessness make her the perfect victim of Leuk the Hare's tricks.
fun_fact: Bouki is the essential counterpoint to Leuk in Wolof storytelling. Together they form a classic trickster pair that has counterparts across West Africa. Bouki's repeated failures teach Wolof children the value of patience, careful work, and honesty.
source: Wolof folklore of Senegal — Bouki hyena trickster documented in Senegalese oral traditions.
